text:
the actor uses a fraudulent, but legitimately created LinkedIn proWle to initiate contact with individuals at the targeted company by sending invitations with a short message. This appears as a benign email Adversaries may gather information about the victim's identity that can be used during targeting. Information about identities may include a variety of details, including personal data (ex

often suggests the recipient click on a link Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious link in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.

this actor used an attached PDF with embedded URLs or other malicious attachments. Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious attachment in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.

The landing page initiates a download of a Microsoft Word Wle with malicious macros Tools or files may be copied from an external adversary-controlled system to the victim network through the command and control channel.

If the recipient enables macros, the "More_eggs" payload will be downloaded and executed. An adversary may rely upon a user opening a malicious file in order to gain execution. Users may be subjected to social engineering to get them to open a file that will lead to code execution.

the landing page may initiate the download of a JScript loader instead, but this intermediate malware still ultimately results in the delivery of More_eggs. Adversaries may abuse various implementations of JavaScript for execution.

its ability to download additional payloads, More_eggs has extensive capabilities to proWle the infected machine Tools or files may be copied from an external adversary-controlled system to the victim network through the command and control channel.

its ability to download additional payloads, More_eggs has extensive capabilities to proWle the infected machine Adversaries may search local system sources.

text:
in the initial phase of an intrusion using stolen credentials stolen credentials were used in the initial phase of the intrusion

Cobalt Strike, Metasploit, and publicly available tools such as Adfind and 7-Zip to conduct internal reconnaissance, compress data, and aid their overall mission these tools were used to conduct internal reconaissance

Cobalt Strike, Metasploit, and publicly available tools such as Adfind and 7-Zip to conduct internal reconnaissance, compress data, and aid their overall mission 7-Zip was used to compress data

Pivoting from these initial leads, analysts identified suspicious SMB connections and Windows Registry artifacts that indicated the attacker had installed malicious Windows services to execute PowerShell commands on remote systems. SMB connections were used.

Pivoting from these initial leads, analysts identified suspicious SMB connections and Windows Registry artifacts that indicated the attacker had installed malicious Windows services to execute PowerShell commands on remote systems. it was used, togheter with ID.004, to install malicious Windows services.

Pivoting from these initial leads, analysts identified suspicious SMB connections and Windows Registry artifacts that indicated the attacker had installed malicious Windows services to execute PowerShell commands on remote systems. it was used, togheter with ID.004, to install malicious Windows services. These services execute PowerShell commands on remote systems.

Pivoting from these initial leads, analysts identified suspicious SMB connections and Windows Registry artifacts that indicated the attacker had installed malicious Windows services to execute PowerShell commands on remote systems. PowerShell commands were used on remote systems.

FIN6 compromised an internet facing system. to initially gain access to the environment, FIN6 compromised and internet facing system.

FIN6 leveraged stolen credentials to move laterally FIN6 used stolen credentials to access system and move laterally within the environment

using the Windows' Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) FIN6 exploited the RDP protocol to move laterally within the environment using stolen credentials

FIN6 used PowerShell to execute an encoded command. Use of PowerShell to execute an encoded command

The command consisted of a byte array containing a base64 encoded payload FIN6 used base64-encoded payloads to obfuscate the malicious content and make it less obvious to security tools.

The encoded payload was a Cobalt Strike httpsstager that was injected into the PowerShell process that ran the command. Cobal Strike httpsstager injected into the PowerShell process

The Cobalt Strike httpsstager was configured to download a second payload from hxxps://176.126.85[.]207:443/7sJh. Cobalt Strike httpsstager configured to download a second payload

it was a shellcode payload configured to download a third payload from hxxps://176.126.85[.]207/ca Cobalt Strike httpsstager configured to download a third payload

FIN6 also leveraged the creation of Windows services (named with a random 16- character string such as IXiCDtPbtGWnrAGQ) to execute encoded PowerShell commands Creation of Windows services with random 16-character names

The encoded command contained a Metasploit reverse HTTP shellcode payload stored in a byte-array like the first technique Metasploit revere HTTP shellcode payload

The Metasploit reverse HTTP payload was configured to communicate with the command and control (C2) IP address 176.126.85[.]207 with a randomly named resource such as "/ilX9zObq6LleAF8BBdsdHwRjapd8_1Tl4Y- 9Rc6hMbPXHPgVTWTtb0xfb7BpIyC1Lia31F5gCN_btvkad7aR2JF5ySRLZmTtY" over TCP port 443. Communicate with the C2 IP address over TCP port 443

This C2 URL contained shellcode that would make an HTTPS request for an additional download.

To achieve privilege escalation within the environment, FIN6 utilized a named pipe impersonation technique included within the Metasploit framework that allows for SYSTEM-level privilege escalation FIN6 useD a named pipe impersonation technique included in the Metasploit framework to obtain SYSTEM level privileges.

FIN6 conducted internal reconnaissance with a Windows batch file leveraging Adfind to query Active Directory Internal reconnaissance using Adfind to query Active Directory

7-zip to compress the results for exfiltration

FIN6 was able to identify user accounts that could access additional hosts in the domain Identify user accounts that could access additional hosts in the domain.

FIN6 used another set of compromised credentials with membership to additional groups in the domain to RDP to other hosts Use of compromised credentials to access additional systems.

FIN6 used another set of compromised credentials with membership to additional groups in the domain to RDP to other hosts Using RDP protocol to move laterally between systems using compromised credentials.

FIN6 used encoded PowerShell commands to install Cobalt Strike on compromised systems. Encoded PowerShell commands to install Cobalt Strike.

The attacker made use of Cobalt Strike's "psexec" lateral movement command to create a Windows service named with a random 16-character string on the target system and execute encoded PowerShell.

In some cases, the encoded PowerShell commands were used to download and execute content hosted on the paste site hxxps://pastebin[.]com. Encoded PowerShell commands to download and execute content from Pastebin

FIN6 also moved laterally to servers in the environment using RDP Moved laterally using RDP

configured them as malware "distribution" servers Configured servers as malware distribution servers

The distribution servers were used to stage the LockerGoga ransomware, additional utilities, and deployment scripts to automate installation of the ransomware. Staging LockerGaga for the installation

The distribution servers were used to stage the LockerGoga ransomware, additional utilities, and deployment scripts to automate installation of the ransomware. Preparation of distribution and utility scripts for later transfer and use.

Mandiant identified a utility script named kill.bat that was run on systems in the environment. This script contained a series of anti-forensics and other commands intended to disable antivirus and destabilize the operating system. Utility script with anti-forensic commands to remove indicators on compromised systems.

Mandiant identified a utility script named kill.bat that was run on systems in the environment. This script contained a series of anti-forensics and other commands intended to disable antivirus and destabilize the operating system. Disabling antivirus and destabilizing the operating system using kill.bat commands.

FIN6 automated the deployment of kill.bat and the LockerGoga ransomware using batch script files. Using batch scripts to automate ransomware deployment and kill.bat.

These BAT files contained psexec commands to connect to remote systems and deploy kill.bat along with LockerGoga Use of psexec to run services and scripts on remote systems.

These BAT files contained psexec commands to connect to remote systems and deploy kill.bat along with LockerGoga Use of psexec for lateral movement via Windows administrative shares.

FIN6 renamed the psexec service name to "mstdc" in order to masquerade as the legitimate Windows executable "msdtc." Renamed psexec service name to "mstdc" to masquerade as legitimate Windows executable "msdtc"

Domain administrators have complete control over Windows systems in an Active Directory environment Used compromised domain administrator credential

First technique

Second technique

TRINITY POS (aka FrameworkPOS)

FIN6 targets companies, using phishing emails in an attempt to infect the targeted victim's network
Using phishing emails to infect the targeted victim's network

FIN6 actors targeted multiple high value eCommerce merchants with malicious documents that contained links to a malicious server
Malicious documents containing links to a malicious server

that allowed the execution of PowerShell scripts, granting the attackers direct access to the merchants' networks.
Using PowerShell scripts to gain direct access to merchant networks.

The FIN6 actors were able to utilize this access to move through the merchant's networks to access the eCommerce environment, and specifically the payment servers.
Discovery of remote systems to identify eCommerce payment environments and servers

use of malicious documents to infect victims
Malicious documents to infect victims

malware known asMore_Eggs (aka Terra Loader)
Infect victims with malware known as More_Eggs (aka Terra Loader)

that is used to launch malicious scripts on the infected machine.
Launch malicious scripts on the infected machine

FIN6 establishes a foothold within a victim's environment by leveraging various payloads, such as components of the Metasploit Framework (e.g., Meterpreter,JSPSPYand CmdSQL)
Leveraging various payloads such as Metasploit Framework components (e.g., Meterpreter, JSPSPY, and CmdSQL)

or

specialized version of theAMMYYremote administration tool (RAT) known as FlawedAmmyy
Specialized version of the AMMYY remote administration tool (RAT) known as FlawedAmmyy

attackers then establish persistent access to the victim's network using Meterpreter related code, allowing the attackers to maintain a remote connection to the infected system.
Establishing persistent access using Meterpreter related code

FIN6 uses legitimate publicly available tools to map the internal network and conduct reconnaissance
Using legitimate publicly available tools to map the internal network and conduct reconnaissance

the actors gather information on systems running SQL Database Server instances and dump schemas for multiple databases and SQL user accounts
Gathering information on systems running SQL Database Server instances and dumping schemas for multiple databases and SQL user accounts.

The actors also target network credentials using a malware family known asStealer One(see below in the sectionTechnical Analysis and Indicators ofCompromise).
Targeting network credentials using a malware family known as Stealer One

FIN6 employs legitimate administrative tools,
Employing legitimate administrative tools

Microsoft's built-in SQL querying tool
Use of native tools such as osql.exe to perform SQL queries.

AdFind (a free command-line tool for querying Active Directory)
AdFind used to query Active Directory

ProcDump (a Microsoft SysInternals command-line utility used to perform analysis of running processes).
ProcDump for analysis of running processes.

WindowsCredentials Editor
Collection of system credentials using Windows Credentials Editor

Metasploit-related tools
Using Metasploit-related tools to exploit vulnerabilities and gain elevated privileges.

Metasploit-related tools
Using Metasploit tools for remote access and privilege escalation.

FIN6 relies on credentials stolen from various systems (e.g. usernames and password hashes) for lateral movement
Using valid accounts for lateral movement

FIN6 relies on credentials stolen from various systems (e.g. usernames and password hashes) for lateral movement
Collection of system credentials, such as usernames and password hashes.

Investigations show that FIN6 relies on PowerShell scripts and Meterpreter based shells to maintain access to and move laterally through the network (see below in the sectionTechnical Analysis and Indicators ofCompromise).
Using PowerShell scripts to maintain access and move laterally through the network

Investigations show that FIN6 relies on PowerShell scripts and Meterpreter based shells to maintain access to and move laterally through the network (see below in the sectionTechnical Analysis and Indicators ofCompromise).
Using Meterpreter-based shells to maintain access and move laterally through the network

The malware checks network connectivity by sending an HTTP HEAD (GET in some new versions) request to the following legitimate URL: •hxxp://www[.]w3[.]org/1999/XSL/Format
the malware use HTTP HEAD to check network connectivity

Magecart operators registered a domain called neweggstats.com with the intent of blending in with Newegg's primary domain, newegg.com Adversaries may acquire domains that can be used during targeting. Domain names are the human readable names used to represent one or more IP addresses. They can be purchased or, in some cases, acquired for free.
Adversaries may impersonate a trusted person or organization in order to persuade and trick a target into performing some action on their behalf.
a Magecart drop server where their skimmer backend runs to receive skimmed credit card information Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over an existing command and control channel.
these actors acquired a certificate issued for the domain by Comodo to lend an air of legitimacy to their page Adversaries may buy and/or steal SSL/TLS certificates that can be used during targeting. SSL/TLS certificates are designed to instill trust.
the attackers placed the skimmer code on Newegg Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries.
The skimmer was put on the payment processing page itself, not in a script, so it would not show unless the payment page was hit. Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover.

text:
FrameworkPOS scraping malware installed on some of the thin clients.
initializing PowerShell/WMI stages that downloaded and reflectively loaded Cobalt-Strike beacon with PowerShell extension directly into the memory.
Downloading a payload such as the Cobalt Strike beacon.
Loading code (such as Cobalt Strike beacon) directly into memory without writing it to disk.
Using PowerShell extensions to load the Cobalt Strike beacon into memory.
the Cobalt Strike beacon payload gives attackers full control over the infected system.
the ability to move laterally to other systems.
harvest user credentials.
evading advanced EDR scanning techniques.
one vector is executed through HTA files.
that execute PowerShell scripts.
part of an embedded VBScript.
some of them are executed through WMI.
base64 encoded script.
that is either remotely injected into the existing 32 bit or into a newly created 32 bit PowerShell process (if the current PowerShell is 64bit).
The injected shellcode is a regular Metasploit downloader shellcode.
that traverses the PEB, resolves the function names by the standard ROR 13 hash.
downloads the next stage shellcode directly into memory from the C2.
2 types.
regular direct reflective loaded Cobalt Strike DLL beacon, usually XOR encoded.
shellcode backdoor beacon with PowerShell and Mimikatz functionality.
install "WindowsHelpAssistant" task in the task scheduler.
uses rundll32.exe with System privileges to execute and export function "workerInstance" from a downloaded binary DLL "Assistant32.dll".
the first one.
The second type.
similar command execution as part of the HKLM Run key.
The malware XOR's (xor 0xAA) the credit card information before exfiltration through DNS tunneling.

text:
MAZE ransomware was initially distributed directly via exploit kits.

Mandiant observed multiple email campaigns delivering Maze ransomware.

These emails used tax, invoice, and package delivery themes with document attachments or inline links to documents which download and execute Maze ransomware.

These emails originated from a compromised or spoofed account.

These emails used the subjects "Missed package delivery" and "Your AT&T wireless bill is ready to view" to induce victims to open the messages.

were sent using a number of malicious domains with the registrant address abusereceive@hitler.rocks.

A user downloaded a malicious resume-themed Microsoft Word document that contained macros.

launched an IcedID payload, which was ultimately used to execute an instance of BEACON.

An actor logged into an internet-facing system via RDP.

The account used to grant initial access was a generic support account.

An actor exploited a misconfiguration on an Internet-facing system.

An actor logged into a Citrix web portal account with a weak password.

launch a Meterpreter payload on an internal system.

The use of legitimate credentials and broad distribution of BEACON across victim environments.

an actor create their own domain account to enable later-stage operations.

installing BEACON payloads on many servers and workstations.

Web shells were deployed to an internet-facing system.

Intrusion operators regularly obtained and maintained access to multiple domain and local system accounts with varying permissions that were used throughout their operations.

An actor created a new domain account and added it to the domain administrators group.

MAZE intrusion operators employed Mimikatz to collect credentials to enable privilege escalation.

use of Bloodhound, and more manual searches for files containing credentials.

The actor attempted to find files with the word "password" within the environment.

The actor attempted to identify hosts running the KeePass password safe software.

Actors primarily used Procdump and Mimikatz to collect credentials used to enable later stages of their intrusion.

Bloodhound and PingCastle were also used, presumably to enable attackers' efforts to understand the impacted organization's Active Directory configuration.

In this case the responsible actors also attempted to exfiltrate collected credentials to multiple different cloud file storage services.

The responsible actor executed a large number of reconnaissance scripts via Cobalt Strike to collect network, host, filesystem, and domain related information.

Multiple built-in Windows commands were used to enable network, account, and host reconnaissance of the impacted environment.

the actors also supplied and used Advanced IP Scanner and Adfind to support this stage of their operations.

a batch script named '2.bat' which contained a series of nslookup commands.

FTP server.

via an encoded PowerShell script.

PowerSploit/PowerView.

a reconnaissance script designed to enumerate directories across internal hosts.

An actor employed the adfind tool and a batch script to collect information about their network, hosts, domain, and users.

The output from this batch script (2adfind.bat) was saved into an archive named 'ad.7z' using an instance of the 7zip archiving utility named 7.exe.

An actor collected directory listings from file servers across an impacted environment.

Cobalt Strike.

they also tunneled RDP using the ngrok utility.

employed tscon to hijack legitimate rdp sessions to enable both lateral movement and privilege escalation.

Stolen credentials were then used to move laterally.

across the network via RDP.

Metasploit.

using a local administrator account.

EternalBlue.

MAZE.

a base64-encoded PowerShell script.

a predefined FTP server using a hard-coded username and password.

A different base64-encoded PowerShell command.

Actors deploying MAZE ransomware have also used the utility WinSCP to exfiltrate data to an attacker-controlled FTP server.

An actor has been observed employing a file replication utility and copying the stolen data to a cloud file hosting/sharing service.

threat actors employed the 7zip utility to archive data from across various corporate file shares.

These archives were then exfiltrated to an attacker-controlled server via FTP using the WinSCP utility.

Five days after data was exfiltrated from a victim environment the actor copied a MAZE ransomware binary to 15 hosts within the victim environment and successfully executed it on a portion of these systems.

Attackers employed batch scripts and a series to txt files containing host names to distribute and execute MAZE ransomware on many servers and workstations across the victim environment.

using a domain administrator account created earlier in the intrusion.

Each of these batch scripts contained a series of commands that employed the copy command, WMIC, and PsExec to copy and execute a kill script (windows.bat) and an instance of MAZE ransomware (sss.exe) on hosts across the impacted environment.

Notably, forensic analysis of the impacted environment revealed MAZE deployment scripts targeting ten times as many hosts as were ultimately encrypted.

text:
FIN6 relied on Windows' Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) to move laterally across affected networks

two different techniques

This movement enabled FIN6 to then inject LockerGoga and Ryuk ransomware

executing an encoded command through PowerShell

The command downloaded a Cobalt Strike payload

which in turn was configured to drop a malicious payload onto the systems

use of a Windows Service created by Metasploit

that was used to communicate with a C2 server for dropping additional payloads into compromised systems.

This would allow FIN6 to escalate privileges in the system.

first technique

second technique

Email phishing campaigns aimed to credential theft.
Adversaries may obtain credentials of existing accounts.
FIN6 used a Metasploit PowerShell module to download and execute shellcode and to set up a local listener that would execute shellcode received over a specific port. Similarly, FIN6 used at least two downloaders called HARDTACK and SHIPBREAD (apparent variations on Metasploit payloads) to establish backdoor access to the compromised environment. Both of these tools are configured to connect to remote command and control (CnC) servers and download and execute shellcode. Tools or files may be copied from an external adversary-controlled system to the victim network through the command and control channel.
Adversaries can abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution.
FIN6 generally used either registry run keys Adversaries may achieve persistence by adding a program to a startup folder or referencing it with a Registry run key.
Windows scheduled tasks in order to establish persistence for these tools. Adversaries may abuse task scheduling functionality to facilitate initial or recurring execution of malicious code.
FIN6 used additional public utilities such as Windows Credentials Editor for privilege escalation and credential harvesting Adversaries may attemp to dump credentials to obtain account login.
FIN6 also used Metasploit's PsExec NTDSGRAB module to obtain a copy of the Active Directory database (ntds.dit). Access to this file would allow them to extract password hashes from the file and crack them offline. Adversaries may attempt to access or create a copy of the Active Directory domain database in order to steal credential information.
Adversaries may use password cracking to attempt to recover usable credentials, such as plaintext passwords, when credential material such as password hashes are obtained.
In addition to collecting credentials, FIN6 used publicly available tools to map the internal network and conduct reconnaissance against Active Directory, Structured Query Language (SQL) servers and NetBIOS. FIN6 used publicly available tools (including Microsoft's built-in SQL querying tool, osql.exe) to map the internal network and conduct reconnaissance against Active Directory, SQL servers, and NetBIOS.
using credentials stolen from various systems on which they gathered usernames and password hashes Adversaries may attempt to dump credentials to obtain account login and credential material, normally in the form of a hash or a clear text password.
remote command execution tools such as PsExec and Remote Command Executor (RemCom) throughout the rest of the lateral movement phase. Adversaries may gain access to and use centralized software suites installed within an enterprise to execute commands and move laterally through the network.
FIN6 leveraged the publicly available Plink command-line utility (part of the PuTTY SSH and Telnet suite) to create SSH tunnels to CnC servers under their control Adversaries perform SSH tunneling.
they used these SSH tunnels to route Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) traffic and allow for interactive RDP sessions with systems in the target network. Adversaries may use Valid Accounts to log into a computer using the Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP). The adversary may then perform actions as the logged-on user.
Once the malware identifies track data, it copies and encodes it to a local file in a subdirectory of the c:\windows\ directory while attempting to conceal these files with .dll or .chm extensions. Adversaries may target and collect data from local system sources.
Adversaries may attempt to manipulate features of their artifacts to make them appear legitimate or benign to users and/or security tools.
The malware encodes the data with a simple substitution cipher and single-byte XOR using the OxAA key. Encrypting and/or encoding file content aims to conceal malicious artifacts within a file used in an intrusion.
they then copied the stolen data to external CnC servers under their control using the FTP command line utility Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over an existing command and control channel.
FIN6 used an alternative extraction method to upload payment card data to a public file sharing service. Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over a different protocol than that of the existing command and control channel. The data may also be sent to an alternate network location from the main command and control server.
to move the stolen payment card data out of the environment, FIN6 used a script to systematically iterate through a list of compromised POS systems, copying the harvested track data files to a numbered "log" file before removing the original data files. They then compressed the log files into a ZIP archive and moved the archive through the environment to an intermediary system and then to a staging system.
gathered information on systems running SQL instances, dumping schemas for multiple databases and SQL user accounts

The attackers injected their code into a library provided on Volusion's e-commerce platform for their client shops.

They created a new object "e.widget.unbridge" right before the original object "e.widget.bridge" and the malicious code is inside the new object. It's worth noting that the attackers even wrote their malicious code in a similar coding style to make it look more like a part of the original source code.

The injected object will create a new script element to load another remote script stored on Google Storage.

The skimmer copies the information on the entire payment form: the victim's name, address, phone number, email address, and credit card details (the number, cardholder name, expiration month, expiration year, and CVV number).

Once the skimmer has the credit card details, it serializes the copied data into a string and encodes it with Base64. Then, it performs a character permutation on the encoded string to make sure it can't be directly decoded with Base64 decoding.

it will use HTTP POST to send the stolen payment information to a remote server at "hxxps[:]//volusion-cdn[.]com/analytics/beacon" for exfiltration.

Magecart injects scripts designed to steal sensitive data that consumers enter into online payment forms on e-commerce websites directly or through compromised third-party suppliers used by these sites Adversaries may abuse various implementations of JavaScript for execution.
Adversaries may use methods of capturing user input to obtain credentials or collect information.

once a user hits the button to submit their payment on the compromised British Airways site, the information from the payment form is extracted along with their name and sent to the attacker's server. Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over an existing command and control channel.

they decided to go with a paid certificate from Comodo instead of a free LetsEncrypt certificate, likely to make it appear like a legitimate server Adversaries may buy and/or steal SSL/TLS certificates that can be used during targeting. SSL/TLS certificates are designed to instill trust.

final_triplets.subject: adversaries
final_triplets.relation: may steal data by exfiltrating it over

text:
targeting specific employees with spear phishing emails advertising fake job advertisements
deploying the More_eggs JScript backdoor malware (aka Terra Loader, SpicyOmelette) Using JavaScript/JScript to execute the More_eggs malware.
This tool, a TTP observed in ITG08 attacks since 2018, is sold on the dark web by an underground malware-as-aservice (MaaS) provider
the use of Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to automate the remote execution of PowerShell scripts
PowerShell commands with base64 encoding,
Metasploit and PowerShell to move laterally and deploy malware.
the threat actor began by targeting handpicked employees using LinkedIn messaging and email
Sending spear phishing emails containing links to malicious payloads.
downloaded a ZIP file containing a malicious Windows Script File (WSF) that initiated the infection routine of the More_eggs backdoor
the ZIP file and WSF files were deleted upon a successful malware infection
a nonmalicious decoy document dropped to the disk drive
The spear phishing attacks
the installation of the More_eggs JScript backdoor
established a reverse shell connection to the attacker's command-and-control (C&C) infrastructure
download and execution of files and scripts and running commands using cmd.exe
the More_eggs backdoor later downloaded additional files, including a signed binary shellcode loader and a signed Dynamic Link Library (DLL)
to create a reverse shell and connect to a remote host
they employed WMI and PowerShell techniques to perform network reconnaissance and move laterally within the environment
The attackers used this technique to remotely install a Metasploit reverse TCP stager on select systems, subsequently spawning a Meterpreter session and Mimikatz
Meterpreter is a payload component in the Metasploit Framework that uses in-memory DLL injection
Mimikatz is a post-exploitation tool that allows attackers to extract credentials from volatile memory
The shellcode loader was observed on one infected device as updater.exe with the Metasploit-style service name APTYnDS1ABEuUHEA, indicating that it was installed as a service
second stage Meterpreter DLL into memory, allowing the attacker to spawn a Meterpreter session via a handler and initiate the loading of extensions, such as Mimikatz
the attacker leveraged in-memory attacks by injecting malicious code, in this case Mimikatz, into legitimate system processes
ITG08 remotely connected to these devices using PowerShell and WMI and downloaded and executed a DLL file,
The process begins with the consistent execution of a malicious DLL using the legitimate regsvr32.exe Windows Utility
The JScript loader file is highly obfuscated and decodes the More_eggs backdoor in a variety of ways, including RC4 decryption
The RC4 key is then used to encrypt data, which is additionally basE91 encoded and sent back to the C&C. BasE91 is a method for encoding binary as ASCII characters
